coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in Carbon Dots/2-D hybrid materials
coredata.dc:creator: Falara, Pinelopi P.
Zourou, Adamantia
Kordatos, Konstantinos V.
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon Dots (CDs) are the latest addition in the carbon nanostructures family. In general, CDs structure consists of a carbon core and a large number of functional groups (
−
OH,
−
COOH,
−
NH2, etc.) on the surface. Due to their unique properties, including small size, photoluminescence, photostability, biocompatibility and low toxicity, CDs could be employed for sensing, photocatalysis, bioimaging, optoelectronic and energy storage applications. In addition, the abundant functional groups on their surface could operate as active coordination sites for interactions with other nanomaterials, producing a large number of CDs-based hybrid materials, including CDs/2-D hybrid materials. This critical review discusses the scientific work published in the literature during the last five years, referring to the combination of CDs with 2-D nanomaterials (graphene-based materials, g-C3N4, TMDCs, LTMOs, LDHs and LDOs), the effects of CDs in 2-D properties and their potential applications in a wide range of scientific/technological areas.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in Carbon Dots/2-D hybrid materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, National Technical University of Athens, 9 Iroon Polytechniou St., Zografou, 15780, Athens, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon Dots (CDs) are the latest addition in the carbon nanostructures family. In general, CDs structure consists of a carbon core and a large number of functional groups (
−
OH,
−
COOH,
−
NH2, etc.) on the surface. Due to their unique properties, including small size, photoluminescence, photostability, biocompatibility and low toxicity, CDs could be employed for sensing, photocatalysis, bioimaging, optoelectronic and energy storage applications. In addition, the abundant functional groups on their surface could operate as active coordination sites for interactions with other nanomaterials, producing a large number of CDs-based hybrid materials, including CDs/2-D hybrid materials. This critical review discusses the scientific work published in the literature during the last five years, referring to the combination of CDs with 2-D nanomaterials (graphene-based materials, g-C3N4, TMDCs, LTMOs, LDHs and LDOs), the effects of CDs in 2-D properties and their potential applications in a wide range of scientific/technological areas.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid advancement of materials science and engineering, as well as the desire for new functions has led to an enormous demand for novel materials. It is known that a large number of the well-established materials, such as metals, ceramics and polymers cannot fulfill all technological desires for the various new applications [1]. In this context, the combination of two or more materials could exhibit superior properties compared to those of the individual counterparts, resulting in the development of hybrid materials. According to IUPAC, a hybrid material is composed of an intimate mixture of inorganic components, organic components, or both types of components [2]. It is interesting to note that hybrid materials have been present even from the time of ancient Maya, where hybrid pigments, such as Maya Blue, were formed [3]. Nowadays, the area of hybrid materials is continuously and rapidly expanding. A basic search on Scopus using the phrase “hybrid AND materials” generates a record of more than 110,000 papers published during the past decade. More specifically, the number of the documents on this topic has more than doubled over the last ten years, as it is presented in Fig. 1 a. In this broad area of hybrid materials, carbon-based hybrid materials are also included. According to Scopus, more than 8700 papers refer to the above topic, indicating that they have gained widespread attention in the scientific community. The significant increase in the number of documents published in 2021 as compared to 2010 could be observed in Fig. 1b.

Generally, the interest of the scientific community for carbon lies in its unique ability to participate in robust covalent bonds with other carbon atoms in diverse hybridization states (sp3, sp2, sp), resulting in the formation of several carbon allotropes, such as diamond (sp3) and graphite (sp2). Although both materials consist of carbon atoms, their properties are not similar; the former is a transparent electrical insulator and the hardest known material, while the latter is a black soft material with remarkable electrical conductivity. The difference in their properties is a result of the way that carbon atoms are connected in each material. Due to this distinctive characteristic of carbon, an explosion of research in carbon allotropes and especially, nano-allotropes, including zero-dimensional (0-D) fullerenes, one-dimensional (1-D) carbon nanotubes and two-dimensional (2-D) graphene, has been witnessed over the past years. Fullerene, the first discovered (1985) carbon allotrope, consists of 60 sp2 carbon atoms arranged in hexagons and pentagons to make a spherical structure, while carbon nanotubes (1991) and graphene (2004) present a different arrangement of sp2 carbon atoms, as it is observed in Fig. 2 . Although these nanostructures present sp2 hybridization, resulting in a number of common properties, they also have some significant differences, including solubility-dispersibility in organic solvents, because of their different shapes and size [4].

Carbon dots (CDs) are the newest member in the family of carbon-based nanomaterials, discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 during the electrophoretic purification of single walled carbon nanotubes [6]. They are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles (Fig. 3 ) consisting of a carbon core (graphitic, amorphous or semi-crystalline) surrounded by a large number of functional groups (e.g., carboxylic acids, amine functionalities, etc.), or polymers chains, depending on the choice of starting materials and preparation procedures. CDs demonstrate excellent aqueous dispersibility, low toxicity and low environmental danger. Furthermore, they are highlighted in terms of high photoluminescence, excellent electron-transfer efficiency, tunable light harvesting range and outstanding photoinduced redox properties [7].

Consequently, CDs possess interesting physicochemical properties which make them ideal candidates for a wide range of applications. Thus, various materials could benefit from their combination with CDs, producing carbon-based hybrid materials with enhanced properties. Based on this assessment, researchers have studied different combinations. This review summarizes the recent progress (last 5 years) in the scientific area of hybrid materials based on CDs and 2-D layered nanomaterials.

In general, 2-D nanomaterials are sheet-like structures (e.g., nanosheets, nanoprisms, nanoplates, nanowalls, nanodisks, etc.), which have undergone enormous research development since the discovery of 2-D graphene. As it is presented in Fig. 4 , they are categorized in graphene-based materials [9], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [10], transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) [11,12], layered transition metal oxides (LTMOs) [13,14], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [15], as well as, layered double oxides (LDOs) [16,17]. 2-D nanomaterials exhibit special properties, including small size, large surface-to-volume ratio [18] and anisotropic physicochemical properties [19]. Due to their ultrathin planar structure, 2-D nanomaterials are promising substrates for loading other nanomaterials [20], including CDs.

However, 2-D nanomaterials present some drawbacks which could limit their application in various fields. The combination of 2-D nanomaterials with CDs is expected to have plenty of advantages in several fields [21]. For instance, graphene-based materials suffer from an intrinsic restacking tendency. In regard to electrochemical applications and their use as electrodes, restacking phenomena could limit ion transport and reduce accessibility to the surface area [22]. Therefore, the introduction of CDs between graphene sheets could decrease agglomeration phenomena, resulting in a higher electrochemical performance [23]. In addition, in the field of water treatment, CDs/2-D hybrids could offer efficient contact with contaminants (e.g., heavy metals) and enhanced adsorption capacity due to abundant surface-confined reactive sites of CDs. Furthermore, the introduction of CDs in g-C3N4 could improve its photocatalytic performance since it accelerates interfacial charge transfer process, reduces recombination rates of charge carriers and widens visible absorbance. Moreover, the combination of CDs with LTMOs (e.g. V2O5) with low specific surface area and low conductivity, could enhance the specific capacitance, resulting in high-performance supercapacitors [21,24,25]. In the present work, the effects of CDs introduction in 2-D nanomaterials are studied.

Although the current scientific work on CDs/2-D hybrid materials is in its infancy it is considered to be a key factor for future developments. Therefore, the aim of this review is not only to present the work on the scientific area of CDs/2-D hybrids published in the literature during the last five years, but also to attract the interest of young researchers for this intriguing area. Briefly, Section 2 provides general information regarding classification, structure, properties, synthesis and applications of CDs in order to provide useful guidance at the basic concept level. Subsequently, Section 3 is devoted to the most recent scientific studies published in the literature in the area of hybrid materials that are formed by combining CDs with different categories of 2-D nanomaterials (graphene-based materials, g-C3N4, TMDCs, LTMOs, LDHs and LDOs). Lastly, Section 4 summarizes conclusions and future perspectives.

CDs are the newest member of carbon-based nanomaterials, with a size of less than 20 nm. Due to the development of synthesis methods, as well as the abundance of precursor materials (e.g., graphite, small organic molecules, polymers, natural materials, etc.), a large family of CDs has been formed. Therefore, it is important to present their classification based on their structure. As it is observed in Fig. 5 , CDs could be divided into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and recently carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [26–29]. In general, CDs consist of a carbon core structure, which could present graphite lattice, amorphous or semi-crystalline form and abundant functional groups or polymer chains on the surface. GQDs are disc-shaped 2-D nanoparticles or small fragments of one or few-layer graphene sheets. They are typically anisotropic with lateral dimensions less than 20 nm and height usually less than five layers of graphene sheets (≃ 2.5 nm). CQDs are multilayer, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a crystalline graphitic core. The term “quantum” refers to nanoparticles possessing delocalized band structures, possibly with some contribution from molecular like and surface states. On the other hand, CNDs are amorphous quasi-spherical nanoparticles that do not show a quantum confinement effect but possess excited states arising entirely from molecular-like species [26]. In addition, CPDs which are the most recent form of CDs possess polymer/carbon hybrid structure consisting of abundant functional groups and polymer chains on the surface and a carbon core [27,28].

All these classes of CDs share a plethora of properties that have attracted the interest of the scientific community, such as high photoluminescence [30], low toxicity [31], excellent water solubility [32], biocompatibility [33], good physicochemical and photochemical stability [34,35] and efficient functionalization. Generally, CDs exhibit light absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region with a long tail extending into the visible range even to the near-infrared (NIR) region. The absorption characteristics of CDs are influenced by the π domains, shape, composition, and surface functional groups, as it is presented in Fig. 6 .

Photoluminescence (PL) is probably the most fascinating feature of CDs; however, its origin still remains one of the most debated aspects. Briefly, CDs present different PL modes, including fluorescence (down-conversion fluorescence and up-conversion fluorescence) and phosphorescence. In general, fluorescence is the most common light-emitting process. The down-conversion fluorescence of CDs has been attributed to the surface state (e.g. surface oxidation degree and surface functional groups) [29,36], the quantum confinement effect [37], the conjugated structures [38], the self-trapped excitons [39], the edge defects [40], the free zigzag sites [29,41] and the multi-emissive centers [42]. Regarding the up-conversion fluorescence, it is an anti-Stokes emission, meaning that the CDs are able to emit light of higher energy than that of the excitation wavelength. Phosphorescence properties of CDs, particularly long-last room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP), have greatly attracted the attention of the scientific community during the last years. So as to accomplish RTP satisfactorily, two requirements should mainly be fulfilled: enhancing spin-orbit coupling through the incorporation of transition metals to support intersystem crossing efficiency and restricting vibration and rotation to suppress non-radiative transitions [43]. It has also been noticed that the PL can be quenched suitably and efficiently by either electron acceptor or electron donor molecules in solution; indicating that besides excellent optical properties, CDs are good electron acceptors, as well as electron donors [44,45].

At this point it is important to note that although traditional quantum dots, such as CdSe, ZnSe, CdTe, ZnS, etc., present superior luminescent properties, they are toxic substances with hazardous effects on human health, therefore CDs could replace them in several application fields [46]. Moreover, doping is an effective method to regulate the intrinsic properties of CDs. Depending on the number of heteroatoms introduced into CDs, they can be divided into single doped and co-doped CDs. The introduction of heteroatoms could affect their electronic structure, resulting in the generation of n-type and p-type carriers. As a consequence both optical and electronic properties could be tuned [47].

Moreover, CDs present excellent aqueous solubility due to the abundance of hydrophilic groups on CDs interface (e.g., oxygen-containing functional groups). However, this property depends on their surface chemistry. Therefore, hydrophobic CDs have also been prepared through partial carbonization of hydrophobic precursors and surface modification by hydrophobic molecules [48].

There are two different synthetic approaches of CDs: (i) the top-down and (ii) the bottom-up (Fig. 7 ). The former procedure is based on the breakdown of larger carbon molecules into small-sized structures, while the latter refers to the polymerization and carbonization of small molecules into CDs through chemical reaction [49]. Top-down approaches generally require harsh reaction conditions, expensive materials/equipment, and long processing times. On the contrary, bottom-up techniques are the most widely used because of superior versatility and accessibility [50,51].

To begin with, top-down mainly include arc discharge, laser ablation and electrochemical methods [53]. As mentioned initially, CDs were discovered by Xu et al. [6] in 2004 during the separation of single-walled carbon nanotubes from the arc discharge soot. More specifically, the researchers oxidized arc discharge soot using HNO3; then they extracted the sediment with NaOH solution and a stable black suspension was obtained. After gel electrophoresis, a fast moving band of highly fluorescent CDs was separated [54]. Synthesis of CDs by laser ablation involves the use of a laser and a carbon target [53] and it was first reported by Sun et al. [33] in 2006. During this process, graphite was exposed under laser radiation from a Nd:YAG (1064 nm, 10 Hz) in a flow of Ar at 900 °C and 75 kPa [55]. In addition, Zhou et al. [56] first reported in 2007 the synthesis of CDs from multi-walled carbon nanotubes through an electrochemical method [55]. The electrochemical preparation was performed in a degassed acetonitrile solution with tetrabutylammonium perchlorate as the supporting electrolyte. The electrolyte solution changed from colorless to yellow and finally to a dark brown color that emitted blue luminescence upon irradiation with an UV lamp [56].

The literature is replete with several studies related to bottom-up techniques and the utilization of small organic molecules as the carbon source to fabricate CDs. Among these techniques, the most common are hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, microwave pyrolysis and ultrasonic treatment [53]. Hydrothermal or solvothermal method is the most commonly used technique for CDs synthesis because of low-cost and easy operation. During this procedure, a solution of organic precursors is sealed in an autoclave where reaction occurs in high temperature (>150 °C) for a relatively long time [51]. For instance, Zhang et al. [57] first reported in 2010 a one-pot solvothermal method to synthesize CDs from ascorbic acid in the presence of ethanol, at 180 °C for 4 h. An improvement in hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis has been achieved by the use of microwaves, resulting in a great reduction of time [51]. Similarly, microwave pyrolysis is a rapid and efficient bottom-up method for CDs synthesis. During this process, CDs are prepared through electromagnetic radiation of the reaction mixture of the precursors. Zhu et al. [58] used first-time in 2013 a domestic microwave oven (500 W) to prepare fluorescent CDs via heating saccharides and polyethylene glycol aqueous solution for 3 min [53]. The ultrasonic synthesis method of CDs is based on the thermal effect of cavitation and ultra-high frequency vibration and it has several advantages, such as low cost and preparation of CDs with uniform surface morphology [59]. During this technique, carbon precursors along with acid, alkali and other oxidants are kept under high ultrasound waves [53].

It is noteworthy that generally the formation of by-products during CDs synthesis cannot be avoided. Therefore, after the CDs preparation, purification processes, such as filtration, centrifugation, dialysis and column chromatography are suggested [60]. However, strongest purification protocols are needed since various publications identified the presence of molecular fluorophores in CDs. Based on this aspect, as well as the avoidance of hazardous chemicals (e.g toxic solvents) and high temperatures, novel strategies have been developed, including mechanochemistry, flow chemistry and laser synthesis in the liquid phase (Fig. 8 ) [51]. Chemical reactions run by mechanical energy such as ultrasonication, which was mentioned above, is a relatively new approach in CDs synthesis. Undoubtedly, dry ball-milling is a novel approach for CDs fabrication; in which carbonaceous precursors and co-reactants are inserted into shaking or rotating jars, containing metal or metal oxide balls. The use of frequencies above 300 rpm and reaction times longer than 28 h results in nanoparticles smaller than 5 nm. The advantages of this process are the absence of heating and solvent, as well as the easy purification of CDs by centrifuge filtration after being prepared. In flow chemistry, reactions occurred in tube reactors, in which the reagents are flown using pumps. As a consequence, reactions take place at a known speed based on the flow rate. Synthesis of CDs by flow chemistry was reported by Lu et al. [61] in 2014 for the first time. The use of microfluidic reactors was effective in the rapid screening of the reaction conditions, while by controlling reactor length, size, shape and flow rate, the control of CDs average size could be achieved. Furthermore, laser synthesis of CDs could also take place in liquid phase; compared to laser ablation in gas phase, this technique is green and low-cost since there is no need for vacuum chambers or gas proof systems. As it is proved, the properties of CDs are influenced by laser fluence, wavelength, spot size, pulse frequency, time and the liquid media, as well [51].

Because of their unique properties, CDs have gained a lot of interest in several scientific fields, such as sensing [62], photocatalysis [63], bioimaging [64], optoelectronic devices [65], energy storage [7], etc.. Table 1 summarizes the recent literature on CDs, which have been utilized in a wide range of applications. In addition, the method and the precursors of synthesis are presented.

Graphene is a 2-D nanomaterial with hexagonal crystalline structure of sp2 carbon atoms and is the basic structure of other 2-D carbon nano-allotropes, including graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) (Fig. 4a). The interest of utilizing graphene in several applications has come due to its high surface area and excellent mechanical, thermal and electrical properties [132]. In addition, graphitic C3N4 (g-C3N4), a semiconductor material with C and N as its primary components, has similar molecular structure to graphene. More specifically, its structure is based on units of triazine (C3N3) or tri-s-triazine/heptazine (C6N7) heterocycles (Fig. 4b). Due to its planar configuration with π-conjugated systems, g-C3N4 has the ability to transport charge carriers. Furthermore, it has high chemical and thermal stability under ambient conditions, strong resistance to acidic and alkaline environments, abundant and low-cost precursors and low toxicity [21,133]. The combination of graphene-based materials and g-C3N4 with the different forms of CDs (CQDs, GQDs, CNDs, CPDs) could enhance their performance in several applications including, sensing, optoelectronics, catalysis or photocatalysis, energy storage, etc. Since the review is focused on the current status of CDs/2-D hybrid materials, herein we present the scientific work on CDs/graphene-based materials, and CDs/g-C3N4 published in the literature the last five years.

To begin with, graphene-based materials face agglomeration phenomena, resulting in the decrease of the specific surface area, which could limit their application in various fields [134], including electrochemical sensing applications. Thus, surface modification with various molecules, including CDs, has been suggested. For instance, Ding et al. [23] prepared a CQDs/GO hybrid material for its utilization as an electrode for the electrochemical detection of uric acid and ascorbic acid. Eco-friendly CQDs, prepared by coke powders, were used as spacers to create gaps between GO sheets, resulting in higher surface area, which consequently increases the electrolyte accessibility and electronic transmission. The hybrid material was prepared through the ultrasonic dispersion of GO into CQDs solution, followed by heating (150 °C, 120 min). Based on cyclic voltammograms (CVs) at different scan rates of GO and CQDs/GO [23], the peak-to-peak potential (ΔEP) of the CQDs/GO is smaller than that of the GO electrode, suggesting the better reversibility and electrochemical activity of the former. This fact indicates the CQDs/GO could be used to fabricate high performance biosensors.

Furthermore, the large number of functional groups on CDs surface is also a key factor in enhancing the performance of graphene in electrochemical sensing. Wang et al. [135] published their study on electrochemical detection of trace Cu(II) using a GQDs/graphene-based electrode. The rich oxygen-containing groups (e.g. -COOH) of GQDs were used to bind Cu(II); the possible mechanism for this electrochemical reaction between Cu(II) and GQDs is presented in Fig. 9 a. As a result, the hybrid material presented high sensitivity and good selectivity because of the fast electron transfer from graphene and high affinity to Cu(II) from GQDs. As for the synthetic route of the GQDs/graphene-based electrode (Fig. 9b), graphene dispersion in dimethylformamide was firstly dropped onto a glassy carbon electrode and then dried in air. Following this, an aqueous dispersion of GQDs, which were prepared through a bottom-up pyrolysis of citric acid, was pipetted onto the graphene layer and dried at ambient condition. Based on this work, several groups of scientists have studied the modification of graphene-based materials with CDs for the detection of heavy metals, including the interesting study of Li et al. [136] on the utilization of N-doped CQDs/GO hybrid in Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions detection, and the recent work of Sabet et al. [137] on green synthesis of CQDs/GO for the elimination of Cd2+ and Pb2+ from water.

rGO aggregation as well as low selectivity in various gases limit its utilization as a gas sensor. Hu et al. [138] first reported the use of CDs/rGO hybrid material in NO2 gas sensing. CDs/rGO was synthesized via a one-pot in-situ process, in which an aqueous mixture of GO, citric acid and ethylenediamine was put into an autoclave and heated at different temperatures for 5 h. Interestingly, the combination with CDs enhanced gas sensing properties by ∼3.3 times compared to bare rGO, while the CDs/rGO could detect NO2 even in low concentration (10 ppb). As they indicated, the superior gas sensing properties could be attributed to the increased hole density on the surfaces of rGO due to the introduction of CDs as well as the formation of all-carbon nanoscale heterojunction at the interface between rGO and CDs with few residual N promoting the charge transfer significantly. CDs/rGO is a promising prospect in other fields including energy storage and photocatalysis.

Graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor, with short lived lifetime heat electron-hole pairs, as well as low absorption of light [139], properties which may restrict the range of its photodetection applications [140]. As it has been suggested, in a CDs/graphene-based photodetector, graphene plays the role of the channel material while CDs act as absorbing media to explore the photoresponse characteristics of the device [141]. Recently, Wu et al. [142] fabricated a UV photodetector based on the N-doped CQDs/graphene hybrid. During the fabrication process (Fig. 10 ) N-doped CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine, while graphene was formed on copper foil by chemical vapor deposition and transferred to low-resistance Si wafers with the help of poly(methyl methacrylate), followed by photolithography, Au sputtering and lift-off procedures. Finally, N-doped CQDs dispersion was spin-coated onto the graphene surface followed by annealing. The photodetector exhibited a negative photoconductivity phenomenon, which is attributed to two competing response mechanisms; the first one is the oxygen adsorption and photodesorption induced negative photoresponse, while the second one is the surface defects in N-CQDs related positive photoconductance. In addition, the enhancement of nonlinear optical properties of graphene has been proposed to promote the practical development of graphene-based photonic and optoelectronic devices. According to Vijesh et al. [143] the third-order nonlinear susceptibility χ(3) (a property which finds applications in optical switching and supercontinuum generation) of CDs/GO hybrid increased (4.624∙10−10 esu) in comparison with GO (5.7∙10−13 esu). Furthermore, results indicated that the increase of CDs concentration in the CDs/GO hybrid, also increased χ(3). A similar work has been published by Zheng et al. [144] incorporating CQDs/GO into organically modified silica gel glasses and studying the nonlinear optical properties.

Heteroatom-doped CDs present good electrocatalytic activity, mostly because of the defects at the surface and edges which are considered as encouraging catalytic sites [145]. Based on the good electrocatalytic properties of CDs, Zhao et al. [146] used them to fabricate a CQDs/graphene catalyst for overall water splitting. The synthetic route involves two steps; the first step is the electrochemical exfoliation of graphite, while in the second step the exfoliated graphene was dispersed into the electrolyte solution and then transferred into an autoclave for solvothermal treatment, followed by annealing to remove high-boiling point organic solvent. As it is known, in the process of electrochemical water splitting, catalysts are required for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to reduce the high overpotential and improve the energy conversion efficiency. The presence of defects and edges in CQDs is beneficial for the HER and OER since they provide numerous active sites for electrocatalytic reactions.

Being very good electron acceptors as well as electron donors, CDs are ideal candidates for energy storage applications, such as supercapacitors and batteries. The use of graphene-based materials combined with conductive polymers (e.g., polypyrrole (PPy)) as electrodes in supercapacitors is very common. However, there are still some issues that remain to be overcome to achieve the desirable performance. In this context, Zhang et al. [147] suggested that the incorporation of CDs between GO and PPy could facilitate electron transportation through the active layer. Moreover, the larger specific surface area of CDs could enhance the interfacial property among GO, CDs and PPy, as well as the multiple interfacial polarization; in other words, there is more surface area for access of electrolyte ions. In their work, CDs/GO hybrid was prepared via a simple hydrothermal treatment (150 °C, 12 h) of GO and CDs mixture. CDs were firstly synthesized via heating an aqueous solution of citric acid and ethylenediamine in a domestic microwave. The final GO/CDs/PPy electrode was fabricated via a modified in situ polymerization method. According to galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves the electrode exhibited a specific capacitance of 576 F∙g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A∙g−1 and excellent cycle stability after 5000 cycles. Similar studies have incorporated CDs into graphene-based electrodes in order to increase their reaction area and provide a rapid ion transport channel. Among them there is the study of Li et al. [148] based on the preparation of CDs/rGO hybrid combined with polyaniline electrode, as well as the study of Hoang et al. [149] with the green zucchini-based CDs/graphene electrode. The former presented a specific capacitance of up to 871.8 F∙g−1 at 0.2 A∙g−1 and very good capacitance retention (72%) after 10000 cycles, while the latter exhibited 374 F∙g−1 at 2 mV∙s−1 specific capacitance and superior cycling stability over 10000 cycles with 93.8% capacitance retention, respectively. It is interesting to note the recent study of Li et al. [150], in which they combined rGO with N,P co-doped CQDs to prepare N,P-CQDs/rGO aerogels with hierarchical porous structure as electrodes of supercapacitors, with a specific capacitance of 453.7 F∙g−1 at 1 A∙g−1 and excellent capacity retention (93.5%) of capacity over 10000 cycles. As they indicated, the improved electrochemical properties of N,P-CQDs/rGO compared to rGO and CQDs/rGO is a synergistic effect of the introduction of CQDs and heteroatom co-doping modification; CQDs could reduce rGO agglomeration and increase the specific surface area, while co-doping of CQDs improved the electronic conductivity of rGO, since they provide more electrons to the π-conjugated graphenic lattice. Therefore, their current work may offer a promising strategy for the development of novel and high-performance CQDs-based aerogels for application in supercapacitors and other energy storage systems. As for the synthesis process, firstly CQDs were prepared by chemical oxidation of graphite, followed by hydrothermal treatment (180 °C, 24 h) of a mixture of CQDs, GO and (NH4)2HPO4, which utilized as both N and P source. Lastly, the N, P-CQDs/rGO aerogel was acquired by freeze-drying.

Recently, Zhang et al. [151] published their intriguing work on the preparation of a CDs/rGO paper (Fig. 11 a) as a flexible anode in potassium-ion batteries. Due to surface functionality of CDs, an excellent interface for intercalations between electrodes and electrolytes is created, providing more active sites for ion insertion and extraction, resulting in enhanced kinetics and increased capacity. As it was mentioned before, the oxygen-containing functional groups from CDs play a key role in attracting metal cations. Based on this unique structure, the CDs/rGO anode exhibited a high capacity of 310 mAh∙g−1 at the current density of 100 mA∙g−1. The charge-discharge profiles of CDs/rGO anode under different current densities are shown in Fig. 11b. Only a slight polarization was observed, indicating that the electrode has excellent ion transmission ability and superior rate capability. Moreover, the CDs/rGO electrode delivered an excellent cycling performance with the extremely high capacity and ultra-long cycle life (Fig. 11c). As for the synthesis procedure, glucose was added into GO dispersion, followed by heating in a domestic microwave for 5 min. The product was then ultrasonically dispersed in deionized water (DI water) and filtered by vacuum using an ordinary filtration membrane. After drying in a vacuum, the paper was peeled off and annealed at 1000 °C under the H2/Ar atmosphere.

Nowadays, g-C3N4 is considered as an emerging 2-D material in photocatalysis owing to its advantageous features, such as good physicochemical stability, nontoxicity and applicable band gap energy of about 2.7 eV [152]. The first study on the utilization of g-C3N4 as a heterogeneous catalyst was presented in 2006 [153]. However, g-C3N4 presents some disadvantages, such as photoexcited recombination, poor visible light absorption, easily poisoned by the produced H2O2, relatively low specific surface area and low intrinsic quantum efficiency, which reduce its photocatalytic performance. In view of these issues, the combination of g-C3N4 with CDs, presenting remarkable physicochemical, optical and electronic properties, is suggested to overcome these drawbacks. In particular, the broad spectrum harvesting, the extraordinary up-converted PL and the outstanding photoinduced electron transfer of CDs result in the full utilization of the solar spectrum. Also, CDs could play the role of electron reservoirs to trap photoexcited electrons, which enhances charge separation efficiencies and consequently the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 [152,154,155].

Recently, Seng et al. [156] prepared a N-CQDs/g-C3N4 hybrid material for methylene blue degradation under LED light irradiation with a 2.6 times increased photocatalytic performance over bare g-C3N4. Based on the photoelectrochemical characterization, including transient photocurrent response experiments (Fig. 12 a), the current density of the N-CQDs/g-C3N4 was significantly higher than pure g-C3N4 indicating that electron migration took place from g-C3N4 to the N-doped CQDs, effectively hindering the recombination of electron-hole pairs. In addition, based on the electrochemical impedance spectra (Fig. 12b) the arc resistance (-Z”) of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 was lower compared to g-C3N4 indicating higher charge transfer capacity. As a result, the incorporation of N-doped CQDs contributes to the effective separation of electron-hole pairs. The photocatalytic mechanism of MB degradation is observed in Fig. 12c; based on scavenging tests, ⦁O2 −, OH⦁ and h+ species were involved in the reaction as reduced degradation efficiencies were observed in their absence. As for the synthetic procedure of the N-CQDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 12d), bulk g-C3N4 was firstly prepared by a thermal treatment of urea, followed by exfoliation via ultrasonication in order to delaminate the 2-D layered structure. Simultaneously, N-doped CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea. Then, an aqueous dispersion of the two samples was sealed in an autoclave (120 °C, 4 h).

Similar studies on the utilization of CDs/g-C3N4 in photocatalytic degradation of organic molecules are also present in the literature. Among them, Khaneghah et al. [24], described the utilization of CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for the photodegradation of methylene blue, rhodamine B, fuchsine, phenol organic contaminants, as well as for the photoreduction of Cr(VI). The photocatalytic ability of hybrid material found to be 14.5, 34.7, 20.7, 11.6, and 40.9 times superior in comparison with the pristine g-C3N4 for degradation of methylene blue, rhodamine B, fuchsine, phenol degradation and photoreduction of Cr(VI), respectively. In their work, the hybrid material was simply fabricated by mixing CDs (prepared by citric acid and urea) and g-C3N4 (prepared by melamine precursor) followed by sonication and drying at 80 °C. Moreover, CQDs/g-C3N4 has also been used for the photodegradation of sulfadiazine and 4-bromophenol, by Duan et al. [158] and Sahu et al. [159], respectively. In another work conducted by Tai et al. [109] CQDs/g-C3N4 was used in the photodegradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol, a hazardous pollutant which is responsible for various negative effects; interestingly after 75 min of sunlight irradiation full degradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol was achieved. Human fingernails were used as a carbon source for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs, which were mixed with g-C3N4 and stirred for 24 h, followed by a hydrothermal treatment (100 °C, 2 h). In their interesting work, Hasija et al. [157] synthesized a quaternary composite material consisting of immobilized AgI/ZnO/CQDs on P-doped g-C3N4 as a photocatalyst for the efficient photodegradation of 2,4-dinitrophenol. Almost total mineralization of the pollutant was achieved after 2 h of visible light illumination and the photocatalyst showed great stability and recyclability with 89% efficiency after ten experimental cycles. In a typical synthesis procedure (Fig. 13 a) P-doped g-C3N4 was prepared by condensation of urea and (NH4)2HPO4, under high temperature in a muffle furnace, while CQDs were fabricated through a green hydrothermal treatment of bamboo leaves. The final AgI/ZnO/CQDs/P-doped g-C3N4 photocatalyst (Fig. 13b), was prepared by mixing all the precursors under ultrasonication.

As it was previously mentioned, the key role of CDs in g-C3N4 is the enhancement of light absorption and the separation of electron-hole pairs, which also promote other photocatalytic activities, beyond degradation of organic pollutants. Among them, the most significant are CO2 reduction and water splitting. For instance, Ong et al. [160] reported the electrostatically attached CNDs onto the surface of protonated g-C3N4 through a hydrothermal method for CO2 reduction. Briefly, the bulk g-C3N4 was delaminated into 2-D nanosheets via an ultrasonication-assisted process, followed by the protonation of g-C3N4 by HCl. CNDs were synthesized by the NaOH-assisted ultrasonic treatment of glucose. The hydrothermal synthesis of CNDs/g-C3N4 took place in an autoclave at 120 °C for 4 h, where different CNDs proportions were used. Based on the UV–Vis absorption spectra, there is an increase in the visible light absorption intensity of the CNDs/g-C3N4 compared to pure g-C3N4, which was further surged by increasing CNDs loading. In addition, measurements of the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 in the presence of H2O vapor were conducted under visible light and simulated solar light illumination for 10 h. For all the photocatalysts studied, there were two major products produced by the CO2 reduction, CH4 and CO. However, the rates of CH4 and CO production with CNDs/g-C3N4 were significantly higher than those obtained with pure g-C3N4. In the proposed photocatalytic mechanism of the reduction of CO2 to CH4 and CO under visible light or simulated solar light, the separated photoinduced electrons and holes enhance the reduction of CO2 in the presence of H+. Recently, an interesting study conducted by Li et al. [161] reports the enhanced CH4 selectivity in CO2 photocatalytic reduction over CQDs decorated and oxygen doping g-C3N4. In this work, CQDs decoration and oxygen atom doping were applied to g-C3N4 by a facile one-step hydrothermal method, in which g-C3N4 was added into a mixed liquid precursor of CQDs composed of ethanol and hydrogen peroxide. According to their results, the CH4 production was 14 times improved compared to g-C3N4. The incorporated CQDs not only facilitate charge transfer and separation, but also provide alternative CO2 adsorption and activation sites. Simultaneously, oxygen doping of g-C3N4 leads to a more positive valence band potential and abundant defects, facilitating dissociation of H2O and providing more H+. In a more recent work of Wang et al. [162], microwave-synthesized CDs from urea and citric acid precursors were dissolved with dicyandiamide in dimethylformamide, followed by thermal polymerization at 500 °C and exfoliation to obtain the final CDs/g-C3N4. The hybrid material was utilized in the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to methanol; the photocatalytic activity of CO2 in pure water was evaluated under 1 bar of CO2 at room temperature under visible-light irradiation. Herein, CDs play the role of hole acceptors, preventing the adsorption of the reduction product methanol; hence favorably oxidizing water instead of methanol and enhancing the selectivity of CO2 reduction to alcohols.

Various studies have indicated that the decoration of g-C3N4 with CDs could provide an efficient procedure for H2 generation. Among them, a recent work of Mou et al. [163] reports that the H2 evolution rate under visible light irradiation of N-doped GQDs/g-C3N4 was nearly 7.7 times more than pristine g-C3N4 (Fig. 14 a). The hybrid material was prepared by thermal polycondensation of pre-synthesized N-doped GQDs and urea. The deamination coupling between N-doped GQDs and g-C3N4 moieties creates new energy bands, providing a unique π (N-GQDs) - p (lone pair electrons in ternary N) - π (g-C3N4nanosheet) network. The π-p-π connection units greatly shorten the charge transfer distance and increase the spatial separation efficiency. The proposed routine for electron-hole separation and transport in N-GQDs/g-C3N4 is presented in Fig. 14b. Similar studies on the H2 evolution using photocatalysts based on 2-D g-C3N4 and CDs have been published in the literature during the last five years from other groups, such as Chen et al. [164] and Zhang et al. [165].

Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) with MX2 structure, where M is a transition metallic element (e.g. Mo, W, V, Zr, Re, Ta, Ti) and X is a chalcogenide element (S, Se or Te), are layered materials with strong in-plane bonding and weak out-of-plane van der Waals interactions which enable the exfoliation into 2-D layers of single unit cell thickness [166]. Although TMDCs have attracted a great deal of interest due to their unique properties and earth abundance [167], they face some challenges related to their structure and properties, such as easy restacking, sluggish charge dynamics, inefficient separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers [12,168]. So far, it has been shown that the combination of TMDCs with CDs offers heterojunctions with superior characteristics, which make them desirable for electrochemical systems, optical and electronic devices, as well as photocatalysis [169].

In another study, Han et al. [173] modified MoS2nanosheets with CQDs for effective bacteria-killing. More specifically, bacteria-killing is a process which could be achieved through the production of heat and reactive oxygen species (ROSs) under light irradiation. Although MoS2 could produce ROSs under visible irradiation of 660 nm, this is not enough for effective-bacteria killing due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in 2-D MoS2. As a result, CQDs could transport the photogenerated electrons produced by MoS2 photocatalyst, preventing the recombination of electrons and holes, resulting in a much higher photocatalytic performance (Fig. 17 a). In this work, N-doped CQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using citric acid and glycine as precursors. The as-prepared N-doped CQDs were subsequently added into a MoS2 solution and mixed by ultrasonication (Fig. 17b). The CQDs/MoS2 nanohybrid presented antibacterial ratios of 99.38% and 99.99% against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, respectively. In literature there are similar studies on the utilization of CDs/MoS2 in other photocatalytic applications, such as degradation of organic dyes. For instance, Li et al. [174] prepared a CDs/MoS2 hybrid via solvothermal treatment (200 °C, 6 h) of N,N dimethyl methanamide and bulk MoS2 (Fig. 18 a), for the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue. Based on their results (Fig. 18b) methylene blue degraded quickly with a rate of 91.1% within 35 min upon irradiation. In addition, the group of Chen et al. [175] has also studied the degradation of methylene blue by CDs/MoS2 combined with Fe3O4 to provide magnetic recycling and synergetic co-catalytic activity. The photocatalysis of methylene blue as a function of time by bulk MoS2, layered MoS2 and CDs/MoS2/Fe3O4 under sunlight irradiation is shown in Fig. 18c, based on the photocatalytic mechanism presented in Fig. 18d; the CDs/MoS2/Fe3O4 decomposed 96.8% of the organic dye at 15 min, which was significantly improved compared to both bulk and 2-D MoS2.

In addition, a major drawback of MoS2 and generally layered TMDCs materials, is associated with the tendency to aggregate irreversibly due to van der Waals attractions among sheets. This issue may hinder the use in several applications, including electrocatalysis, due to the decreased number of active sites. Therefore, the incorporation of CDs into MoS2 could decrease agglomeration phenomena. Moreover, the electroactivity of MoS2 is severely diminished because of its intrinsic poor conductivity. Therefore, its combination with CDs results in the enhancement of electrocatalytic activity [176,177]. Zhao et al. [178] proposed a catalyst for HER reaction by combining MoS2 with CQDs. Briefly, CQDs prepared by an electrochemical etching were added to an aqueous solution of Na2MoO4 and l-cysteine. After vigorous stirring, the above mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The CQDs/MoS2 electrocatalyst irradiated by visible light for 30 min exhibited good electrocatalytic activity towards HER with a small overpotential of ∼0.125 V at 10 mA∙cm−2, a high current density, a small Tafel slope of 45 mV/decade and long-term stability in 0.5 M H2SO4. In their recent work, Canton-Vitoria et al. [177] prepared a CDs/MoS2 hybrid and studied its photoelectrocatalytic performance. As for the synthesis procedure, ammonium functionalized MoS2 was combined through strong electrostatic interactions with CDs, which were firstly synthesized by a microwave-assisted polycondensation of citric acid and ethylenediamine, forming aqueous stable CDs/MoS2 ensembles, in which the two components developed electrostatic interactions. The photoelectrocatalytic performance of the CDs/MoS2 heterostructures was evaluated towards HER and was found superior compared to that of MoS2. The enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance could be attributed to the improved proton adsorption capacity due to the aqueous solubility of CDs combined with the presence of the surface functional groups that contribute to draw hydrated protons, as well as the intimate contact between the two components that resulted in improved charge-transfer kinetics. In another work in the field of electrocatalysis, Lv et al. [179] prepared a N-doped CDs/N-doped MoS2 hybrid catalyst in a one-step solvothermal method. The catalyst was evaluated for the electroreduction of CO2 to CO and displayed an excellent performance with high current density (36.2 mA∙cm−2 at −0.9 V), low onset overpotential (130 mV) and a high efficiency (90.2%) for CO formation. The outstanding performance of the hybrid is a result of the good electrical conductivity of N-doped CDs, which accelerated electron transport and thus improved the efficiency of electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CO.

Furthermore, agglomeration phenomena between MoS2 layers decrease the adsorption capacity towards organic pollutants. Wang et al. [180] proposed MoS2 modification with GQDs in order to enhance its surface area and consequently its adsorption properties. More specifically, the GQDs/MoS2 adsorbent prepared by a one-step hydrothermal treatment (200 °C, 12 h) of an aqueous mixture of hexaammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate, thiourea and glucose, was used for the removal of organic dyes from water, under ultrasound assistance. According to N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 19 a), the introduction of GQDs improved the surface area of MoS2 from 54.6 m2 g−1 to 151.4 m2 g−1, since it restricted the overlap of MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. 19b). This also contributes to the increase of active sites for the adsorption of dye molecules. Based on the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 19c) the maximum adsorption capacity of the hybrid material was found to be 285.0 mg∙g−1, 140.0 mg∙g−1 and 210.0 mg∙g−1 for rhodamine B, methyl orange and methylene blue, respectively. In addition, Dong et al. [181] reported for the first time the use of CDs/MoS2 as a sorbent for the solid-phase extraction procedure of brominated flame retardants, while Yilmaz et al. [182] prepared a MoS2/CDs/Fe3O4 magnetic solid-phase extraction adsorbent for the separation and enrichment of ibuprofen from pharmaceutical, environmental water and synthetic urine samples.

Similarly, 2-D tungsten disulfide (WS2), as well as molybdenum diselenide (MoSe2) are TMDCs of particular interest for photocatalytic pollutant degradation. Atkin et al. [169] presented the synthesis of a novel hybrid CDs/WS2 photocatalyst through a simple microwave assisted solvothermal treatment. Firstly, bulk WS2 was exfoliated into 2-D flakes through the dispersion of WS2 powder into ethanol solution followed by sonication. The combination of WS2 with CQDs took place via electrostatic attraction forces. The prepared 2-D WS2 suspension was added to citric acid solution and the mixture was microwaved at 150 °C for 5 min. The prepared CQDs/WS2 hybrid exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance towards the degradation of Congo red, compared to pure WS2. Ren et al. [167] synthesized a CQDs/MoSe2 photocatalyst via a solvothermal method at 180 °C for 12 h, using sucrose, Na2SO3, Se powder, Na2MoO4 and NaBH4 as precursors. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/MoSe2 hybrid material was studied for the reduction of hexavalent chromium Cr(VI). The results showed almost total reduction (99%) of Cr(VI) under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation at 180 min. In a more recent work conducted by Geng et al. [183], CDs/WS2 was also used for NIR-II enhanced photothermal therapy of osteosarcoma and bone regeneration. Based on their results the hybrid exhibited excellent deep-tissue photothermal effect to ablate osteosarcoma under 1064 nm laser irradiation. The synthesis process of CDs/WS2 is shown in Fig. 20 . CDs were prepared via a microwave treatment of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and branched polyethylenimine. Simultaneously, bulk WS2 was first ground in a ball grinder, followed by dispersion in H2SO4 and heated at 90 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation, the products were redispersed in DI water by sonication to obtain WS2 nanosheets. Finally, the CDs/WS2 was synthesized under vigorous stirring of CDs and WS2 nanosheets at room temperature in phosphate buffered saline.

Monolayer TMDCs suffer from low absorption (2–5%) in the visible light region, which induces poor optoelectronic signal output and hinders their application in ultrathin detectors and solar cells. To overcome this drawback, Su et al. [184] proposed the combination of WS2 with CQDs. In literature there is also a study which reports for the first time the combination of monolayer 2-D TMDC tungsten diselenide (WSe2) with N-doped GQDs in order to enhance its photoresponsivity. More specifically, Nguyen et al. [185] prepared a N-GQDs/WSe2 hybrid photodetector (Fig. 21 a) with 480% higher photoresponsivity compared to pristine monolayer WSe2 (Fig. 21b) as a result of the strong light absorption and charge transfer from N-doped GQDs to WSe2. As for the synthesis procedure of N-GQDs/WSe2, briefly, citric acid and urea were hydrothermally treated (160 °C for 3 h), while WSe2 was grown directly onto a SiO2/Si substrate by chemical vapor deposition. Then N-doped GQDs solution was spray casted onto the WSe2 surface at 80 °C on a hot plate.

2-D Layered transition metal oxides (LTMOs) have a relatively longer history compared to other atomically thin materials. They comprise a large number of earth-abundant minerals, while the role of oxygen ions in their structure is the key factor in generating remarkable properties. Due to their specific layered features, electronic structure and unique physicochemical properties, 2-D LTMOs provide a wide spectrum of applications, such as energy storage in batteries and supercapacitors, photocatalysis, sensing, fuel cells, etc. [186].

Among 2-D LTMOs, vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) has the greatest potential for supercapacitor applications. However, it suffers from low conductivity, as well as low specific surface area, which restrict its utilization as an electrode in high-performance supercapacitors. Therefore, further research on V2O5 is necessary. Several studies have reported that conductive CDs could improve the poor conductivity of 2-D LTMOs, resulting in the enhanced specific capacitance. Ganganboina et al. [187] combined V2O5 nanosheets with GQDs via a solvothermal treatment process in order to enhance the electrochemical energy storage properties. As it was indicated, GQDs could serve not only as a conducting component to enhance the electron transfer efficiency in hybrid GQDs/V2O5 hybrid, but also as a toughening agent to minimize the change in volume and thus improve the cyclic stability of the supercapacitor. In their work, both V2O5 nanosheets and GQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method. The V2O5 nanosheets were fabricated by dissolving bulk V2O5 into DI water along with 30% H2O2 under stirring and sealing the solution into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 190 °C for 8 h. Simultaneously, for the preparation of GQDs 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was dispersed into NaOH solution by ultrasonication and the solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated up to 200 °C for 10 h. The final GQDs/V2O5 nanohybrid material was prepared similar to the synthesis of V2O5 nanosheets except for the addition of GQDs into the initial step of the procedure. The results showed that the GQDs/V2O5 electrode exhibited excellent specific capacitance of 572 F∙g−1 at current density of 1 A∙g−1, compared to 227 F∙g−1 of pristine V2O5 nanosheets and superior cycling stability. The increased performances of the electrodes could be explained by the high electronic conductivity provided by the GQDs, while the superior stability is ascribed to the unique morphology and interface structure since GQDs prevent the aggregation of V2O5 and keep the electrode structure stable with enhanced cycling life. V2O5 has been also studied as a promising cathode material for Zn-ion batteries (ZIBs) by Zhang et al. [118] due to the redox states and its variable interlayer spacing, which could provide more diffusion channels for the migration of Zn2+. The interlayer spacing of V2O5 can be expanded by tuning the chemical valence of the V element. More specifically, V(IV) has a bigger ionic radius compared to that of V(V). The favorable V(IV) generated by in situ partial reduction and the introduction of CDs into V2O5 interlayers contribute to an expanded interplanar spacing, as well as improved electric conductivity for fast charge transfer and ion diffusion. In their work, Zhang et al. synthesized CQDs/V2O5 hybrid by a one-step hydrothermal method as a cathode material for ZIBs. In a typical procedure, glucose and V2O5 were dispersed in water to form a suspension by stirring for 20 min and the solution was transferred to a Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 140 °C for 24 h. The CQDs/V2O5 hybrid material presented a high capacity of 460 mA∙h g−1 at 0.1 A∙g−1, excellent rate performance and remarkable long cycling stability. It is worth mentioning that in the literature the combination of V2O5 with CQDs has been also reported for sensing applications. More specifically, Zhu et al. [188] prepared a N-doped CQDs/V2O5 hybrid material as a sensing platform for detection of ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity.

As opposed to V2O5, a large number of studies in the literature is focused on the combination of 2-D manganese dioxide (MnO2) with CDs. CDs/MnO2 hybrid materials have been mostly utilized as fluorescent turn-on probes for the determination of various molecules, including glucose [185,189,190], uric acid [191], ascorbic acid [192,193] glutathione [194,195], dopamine [196], etc. [197–205]. MnO2 nanosheets have several interesting physicochemical properties, such as low toxicity, broad absorption band, high extinction coefficient and rich redox chemistry, which make them a promising sensing platform. The fluorescence of CDs turns off in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets due to the inner filter effect, as well as other quenching mechanisms. When other molecules (e.g. uric acid) are added into the solution of CDs/MnO2, the fluorescence of CDs is partially recovered as a result of molecules reaction with MnO2 nanosheets [206]. Based on this mechanism, which is shown in Fig. 22 , several CDs/MnO2 sensors have been developed. For instance, Gu et al. [190] prepared a CDs/MnO2 turn-on probe for glucose detection. In their work, CDs were prepared by a facile green hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 8 h using peony flowers as the carbon source. For the preparation of MnO2 nanosheets, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide was added into a KMnO4 aqueous solution under stirring. Subsequently, the pH value of the mixture was fixed at 6 with a buffer solution and it was kept stirring at room temperature for 18 h. The resulting precipitate was separated after centrifugation and dried to obtain the final MnO2 nanosheets. Finally, MnO2 nanosheets were dispersed by sonication in ultrapure water followed by mixing with a CDs aqueous solution at room temperature for 10 min. The CDs/MnO2 nanohybrid material showed an excellent linear range from 0.5 to 250 μM with a low detection limit (0.18 μM) for glucose detection. The fluorescence quenching of CDs by MnO2 nanosheets is due to förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Similar studies on the use of CDs/MnO2 in glucose detection have been conducted by Ma et al. [207] and Wang et al. [189] during the last five years.

Besides sensing applications, CDs/MnO2 hybrids have been also used as electrodes for supercapacitors. As it was mentioned before, all 2-D LTMOs face poor conductivity, including MnO2 nanosheets. Therefore, several recent studies [208–210] suggest that MoS2 nanosheets need to be modified with conductive CDs to enhance their electrochemical performance.

Titania (TiO2) is one of the most studied photocatalysts due to its excellent chemical stability and high reactivity under UV light irradiation [211]. 2-D TiO2 nanosheets derived from titanate exfoliation, exhibit similar semiconducting characteristics to the bulk rutile and anatase TiO2, but with slightly larger band gap due to the size quantization [21]. Nevertheless, there still remain some drawbacks in the use of ΤiO2 as a photocatalyst, including the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [212]. These limitations can be resolved by modification of TiO2 with CDs. Li et al. [212] prepared CQDs/TiO2 through a hydrothermal method for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). TiO2 nanosheets were synthesized by a hydrothermal route at 180 °C for 24 h using tetrabutyl titanate and hydrofluoric acid as precursors. TiO2/CQDs photocatalyst was prepared by mixing citric acid solution with TiO2 nanosheets. The above mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. As it was revealed by the results, TiO2/CQDs showed higher photocatalytic performance compared to TiO2 nanosheets, with more than 99.2% Cr(VI) reduction after 120 min irradiation.

Furthermore, due to the lack of functional groups and wide band gap (3.2 eV) with the need of UV light for excitation limits, the application of TiO2 photoelectrochemical sensing toward target molecules is decreased. To achieve high sensitivity and selectivity, it is necessary to develop advanced photoactive materials with high photon-to-electron conversion efficiency and generation rate of electron donors/acceptors. Therefore, CDs with their unique broad-band optical absorption and electronic transmission/reception properties could improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. In their study, Fu et al. [213] prepared a CDs/TiO2 nanohybrid as photoactive material for pentachlorophenol sensing. The enhanced performance could be owing to the synergistic effect between 2-D TiO2 and CDs. The photo-generated electrons from CDs migrate to the conduction band of TiO2 across the interface, while the holes irradiated by visible light could not transfer to the more positive valence band of TiO2, leading to the hindrance of electron-hole recombination. When molecules of pentachlorophenol are captured by the CDs/TiO2 surface, they could be oxidized directly on the surface, and the oxidation product could further avoid electron-hole recombination effectively, thus increasing the photocurrent signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs/2-D layered double hydroxides and CQDs/Layered double oxides
ce:section.ce:para: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are inorganic materials with a 2-D highly tunable brucite-like layered crystal structure, which can be used in a variety of applications because of their low cost, facile synthesis, thermal stability, as well as, chemical versatility. LDHs consist of positively charged metal hydroxides layers with inter-layer anions and water molecules. Their general formula is [M2+ (1−X)M3+ (X) (OH)2]X+(An−)X/n·mH2O, where M2+ and M3+ represent the divalent and trivalent metallic cations, respectively, and An− is an anion, such as OH−, F−, NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2− and SO4 2− [214].

The structure of LDHs endows them with favorable ion-exchange and adsorption capacities for the removal of ionic pollutants from wastewater through ion exchange effect. For example, Fe based LDHs have been reported for the removal of Pb2+ from aqueous solutions. However, they suffer from poor wettability and limited functional groups. Therefore, Ling et al. [215] combined Fe based LDHs with N-doped CQDs in order to enhance their adsorption capacity. The CQDs/FeMg-LDH was synthesized through a solvothermal method. Briefly, Fe(NO3)3·6H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, glucosamine and sodium dodecyl sulfate were dissolved in glycol under stirring. Separately, Na2CO3 was dissolved in glycol. The above solutions were mixed under stirring and the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 24 h. Due to the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of N-doped CQDs, strong interactions with Pb2+ took place. As a result, CQDs/FeMg LDH presented remarkable performance in the removal of Pb2+ from aqueous solution, with very short equilibrium time and a high adsorption capacity of 344.8 mg∙g−1. In another study, Rahmanian et al. [216] reported the combination of ZnAl-LDH with CQDs for the removal of Cd(II) ions from water. The CQDs/ZnAl-LDH nanohybrid was prepared through a process which is illustrated in Fig. 23 . Briefly, N-doped CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal treatment of ammonium citrate at 170 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O and urea (CO(NH2)2) were added to a CQDs ethanol solution followed by vigorous stirring. Then, the solution was transferred to a sealed Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated for 14 h at 140 °C. The obtained CQDs/ZnAl-LDH nanohybrid showed remarkable adsorption ability in the removal of Cd(II) ions from water, due to the enhanced adsorption sites of ZnAl-LDH because of the presence of CQDs in their structure.

Recently, Koilraj et al. [217] used CQDs-decorated MgAl-NO3-LDH as an adsorbent of Sr2+ and SeO4 2−. CQDs were prepared via microwave irradiation using citric acid and urea, dissolved in ultrapure water. Simultaneously, for the synthesis of MgAl-NO3-LDH, a coprecipitation method took place, where a decarbonated solution containing Mg(NO3)2·6H2O and Al(NO3)3·9H2O was pH-adjusted to 10 with the addition of NaOH and NaNO3. The mixture was vigorously stirred under the N2 atmosphere and the obtained slurry was aged at 80 °C for 20 h. Finally, the CQDs/MgAl-NO3-LDH was prepared by the colloidal deposition method. More specifically, MgAl-NO3-LDH was mixed with different amounts of CQDs solution for 2 h, followed by freeze drying for 24 h. For the adsorption measurements, CQDs/MgAl-NO3-LDH was added to a mixed solution containing Na2SeO and SrCl2. The results suggest that CQDs/MgAl-NO3-LDH is an efficient adsorbent for co-immobilization of radioactive nuclides Sr2+ and SeO4 2−.

LDHs materials present low electrical conductivity which restricts their use in electrochemical devices for energy storage applications. Hence, the combination with conductive CQDs would be a desirable solution for improving the electrochemical performance. For example, Wei et al. [218] fabricated a CQDs/NiAl-LDH nanohybrid as pseudocapacitor electrode material. Firstly, CQDs were synthesized through electrochemical ablation of graphite. In a typical process, two identical graphite rods were inserted into the electrolyte as both cathode and anode and static potentials were applied to the two electrodes using a direct current power supply for ten days with stirring. The CQDs/NiAl-LDH nanohybrid material was prepared by a facile one-step solvothermal method. Briefly, the obtained CQDs aqueous solution was dispersed and immersed in ethanol by ultrasonic for 10 min. Then, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O and urea (CO(NH2)2) aqueous solution was added under vigorous stirring for 1 h at room temperature. The above solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, followed by heating at 140 °C for 14 h. The presence of CQDs improved not only the electrical conductivity, but also the surface roughness and the active sites of the hybrid electrode, which further enhanced the electrochemical performance of the CQDs/NiAl-LDH as a supercapacitor electrode material. More specifically, it exhibited superior specific capacitance of 1794 F∙g−1 (at 2 A∙g−1), high-rate capability of 967 F∙g−1 (at 20 A∙g−1) and excellent cycle performance (93% retention over 1500 cycles).

In addition, LDHs have been considered as one of the most efficient oxygen evolution catalysts for photoelectrochemical water splitting into hydrogen and oxygen gas. The electrocatalytic activity and the electrical conductivity of LDHs can be enhanced through the incorporation of CDs in their structure, as it is reported by Chen et al. [219]. In this work, a BiVO4 photoanode modified with CDs/NiFe-LDH was fabricated for solar driven photoelectrochemical water splitting. More specifically, NiFe-LDH was deposited onto BiVO4 electrodes through an electrochemical deposition technique. The NiFe-LDH was deposited at −0.5 V, in sequence in (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 and Ni(NO3)2 aqueous solution for 300 s. Subsequently, CDs were prepared using graphite as both anode and cathode and ultrapure water as electrolyte. Direct current was applied to the two electrodes and the solution was continuously stirred for 120 h. Finally, the NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes were soaked into CDs solution for different times. The resulting CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes presented significantly enhanced photocurrent and charge injection efficiency compared to NiFe-LDH/BiVO4.

LDHs have been also examined as metal-based catalyst supports. For example, Au nanoparticles have been supported over Ni-, Ti- and Cr-based LDHs for alcohol oxidation. The combination of LDHs with CQDs as a catalyst support has been reported for the first time by Guo et al. [220]. In this work, an N-doped CQDs/Mg-Al LDH hybrid was prepared to immobilize Au catalyst for the selective oxidation of benzyl alcohol. Firstly, N-doped CQDs were synthesized through heating of dicyandiamide and citric acid mixture at 180 °C under nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h. Then, the N-doped CQDs/Mg-Al LDH hybrid was assembled by a co-precipitation method, where Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O and N-doped CQDs were put into DI water. The pH of the solution was adjusted at the value of 10.5 using a mixture of NaOH and Na2CO3 and the precipitate was held at 60 °C for 10 h. Finally, the Au nanoparticles supported on the N-doped CQDs/Mg-Al LDH hybrid was synthesized by a deposition-reduction approach, where N-doped CQDs/MgAl-LDH and poly (N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidone) were put into DI water, followed by the addition of HAuCl4 aqueous solution and NaBH4 under stirring. The suspension was stirred for 2 h and the obtained sample was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h. As it was revealed, the Au/N-doped CQDs/Mg-Al LDH hybrid catalyst exhibited enhanced catalytic performance and higher stability compared to Au/Mg-Al LDH, which mainly originated from the strong interactions taken place between N-doped CQDs and Au nanoparticles.

LDHs could be converted to layered double oxides (LDOs) through a calcination process. The calcined LDHs or LDOs [221] present more active sites, higher specific surface area and higher chemical and thermal stability in comparison with pristine LDHs. Therefore, LDOs could be used as adsorbents for the removal of heavy metals, as it is reported by Yao et al. [221]. However, the removal capacity is still significantly limited due to the low amount of surface functional groups. In their study, Yao et al. prepared a CDs/LDO nanocomposite for the removal of U(VI). Firstly, MgCl2·6H2O and AlCl3·6H2O were dissolved in water, under stirring for 3 h. Simultaneously, NaOH and Na2CO3 and chitosan were dissolved in water. Then, the above solutions were mixed under constant stirring, followed by pH adjustment at 9.5. The mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and was firstly maintained at 110 °C for 10 h and then at 80 °C for 8 h. The as-synthesized CDs/Mg-Al LDH was successfully converted to CDs/Mg-Al LDO after calcination at 500 °C for 6 h under N2 conditions. The maximum adsorption capacity of U(VI) for the CDs/Mg-Al LDO was measured to be 354.2 mg∙g−1 at pH = 5.0 and T = 298 K, which was noticeably higher compared to that of pure Mg-Al LDO (237.6 mg∙g−1). The increased adsorption capacity was ascribed to the presence of CDs, because of the enhanced surface area and the rich oxygen functional groups. As a result, the CQDs/Mg-Al LDO adsorbent (Fig. 24 ) could be used for the efficient removal of radionuclides through electrostatic interactions, precipitation, surface complexation and ion exchange.

Similarly, Mg-Al LDO nanosheets coated with B,N-co-doped CDs have been recently used by Zheng et al. [222] for the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) pharmaceutical pollutant under solar light irradiation. B,N-co-doped CDs were selected as the trappers of the photo-excited electron. More specifically, nitrogen atoms play the role of the electron donor, while B atoms increase the amounts of hole-type charge carriers. In a typical procedure, Mg-Al LDH, melamine and boronic acid were added into DI water and ultrasonicated for 20 min, followed by stirring at 60 °C in order to remove DI water, dried at 80 °C for 6 h and calcined at 800 °C for 3 h under N2. Almost total removal of TCH was observed after 90 min of solar light irradiation, while the novel photocatalyst exhibited remarkable photocatalytic stability and durability after five consequent cycle times.

Recent developments regarding CDs, as well as their combination with 2-D nanomaterials have been discussed in this review article. As it was initially mentioned, CDs -the new addition in the field of carbon-based nanomaterials-have attracted the interest of the scientific community because of their unique properties, which render them potential candidates for several applications. More specifically, due to their excellent PL and biocompatibility, CDs constitute one of the most promising nanomaterials to be utilized in sensing and bioimaging applications, including detection of various ions and in vitro imaging of cancer cells, respectively. In addition, because of their high electrical conductivity, long hole-electron lifetimes, ultrafast electron extraction and good chemical stability, they have been used in energy storage devices, as well as optoelectronic devices. Furthermore, photocatalysis is another research area where CDs have entered either as a pure photocatalyst, or as a complementary material to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency. This survey discussed the properties of CDs and reported the work on synthesis and utilization of CDs in the fields of sensing, photocatalysis, bioimaging, fluorescent inks, optoelectronics and energy storage, published in the literature the last five years. The need for new materials with enhanced properties is central to many of the challenges that the world is facing. Thus, the development of nanohybrid materials with desired characteristics is of great importance. In this review, recent work on the combination of CDs with 2-D nanomaterials, including graphene-based nanomaterials, graphitic nitride, TMDCs, LTMOs, LDHs and LDOs was presented, focused on the synthetic strategies that were followed. A variety of techniques were used for the synthesis of CDs/2-D nanohybrids. In some cases, the CDs/2-D nanohybrid was assembled in a one-step process, while in other cases the synthesis of CDs/2-D nanohybrid was completed after two or three steps. Among several techniques, hydrothermal/solvothermal, co-precipitation and electrochemical methods were some of the most frequently observed in the literature. In all studies, it was found that the combination of CDs with 2-D nanomaterials resulted in nanohybrids with enhanced properties, such as photocatalytic performance, adsorption capacity, specific capacitance, etc..

As discussed in this review, during the last years, CDs and CDs/2-D hybrid nanomaterials have attracted great research interest and important developments are anticipated in the field, with the aim to enhance their properties and further expand their applications. Nevertheless, as the research related to CDs and CDs-based hybrids is at an early stage, the consolidation of efficient and repeatable procedures regarding their synthesis and applications is not yet possible. Systematic theoretical research is lacking as recent research and reports focus on the introduction of technical processes. The excellent optoelectronic properties of CDs are widely known but not thoroughly understood; thus, additional research is needed to elucidate the exact parameters that are responsible for their properties (e.g., the effect of size, surface modification and doping in CDs). Furthermore, the use of CDs to modify the existing photocatalysts and photo-electrocatalysts is still limited, however very promising, due to CDs ability of preventing electrons-holes recombination, broadening the visible light harvesting range, introducing more oxygen vacancies and increasing materials’ specific surface area. Simultaneously, 2-D material-based hybrids offer an ideal platform for advanced materials research. It is likely that the research of CDs/2-D nanohybrids based on basic physical properties such as optics, electricity, and magnetism, will still be a research hotspot. To sum up, there is still much to explore as regards CDs and CDs/2-D nanohybrid materials beyond the current state of knowledge in the area. Consequently, additional efforts are necessary to develop new strategies for the synthesis of CDs/2-D nanohybrids and imaginatively, numerous novel functions will be discovered and applications in a wider range of fields during next years.

Indeed, low dimensional nanomaterials including graphene and graphene analogues dispose unique characteristics (almost atomic layer morphology and excellent optical properties), making them ideal candidates for flexible and sometimes transparent electronic/optoelectronic devices disposing high industrialization potential, especially photodetectors, LEDs, solar cells, sensors and quantum cascade lasers [223], where current markets are dominated by conventional thin film semiconductors. However, the efficiency of 2D nanostructures for light detection/emission is limited by their low light absorption ability and the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. The incorporation of CDs presenting excellent local photon capture capabilities promotes the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This can lead to functional, high-quality CDs/2-D heterostructures with improved optoelectronic properties and enhanced performance, providing opportunities for novel applications in the field of nanoelectronics/nanophotonics. For such an endeavor, understanding the intrinsic dynamic mechanisms of various CDs/2-D heterostructures upon photoexcitation is essential, which provides general guidance for the design of materials with better performance in related applications [224]. Powerful time resolved techniques (such as transient photocurrent spectroscopy and ultrafast femtosecond transient spectroscopy) can be jointly used to unravel complicated photoinduced dynamic behavior and the excited state, which are responsible for ultrafast interfacial charge/energy transfer in these heterostructures, thus, permitting important applications in photocatalysis and non-linear optics. An additional important challenge in 2-D nanohybrids is how to improve crystal size, quality and mass production (including large-size, robustness and scalability) strategies to achieve uniform physical and chemical properties for high performance photonic/electronic devices. A new trend is to employ van der Waals heterojunctions, for which the bottom-up fabrication methods (e.g. MBE and MOCVD) may play more crucial roles [225]. In this direction the synergistic coupling at functional CDs/2-D van der Waals heterojunctions formed between CDs and few-layered 2D materials (e.g MoS2, WS2, g-C3N4) might give enhanced performance for electrocatalytic HER, thus unleashing the application potential of establishing these new hybrid materials as non-precious electrocatalysts for energy conversion and storage applications [226].

Pinelopi P. Falara: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Adamantia Zourou: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Konstantinos V. Kordatos: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
